segments.s155-g41: The clause uses the idiom 'رای آمدن' (a counsel/plan comes to someone) where آیدش is 3rd-person present with the enclitic -ش 'to him' (آید + ش). Word order is typical Persian: preposition + noun (به جنگ) indicating direction/purpose, then the impersonal idiom 'رای آیدش' = 'a plan occurs to him', and سازد (3sg) + direct object سپاه follows to mean 'he makes/raises an army'. The conjunction و simply links the two actions.
segments.s155-g42: برکند is a 3rd-person singular present/future sense 'uproot/dislodge/tear away' and takes the phrase روز روشن سیاه as its object/outcome. به من functions as an indirect dative/benefactive ('from me' or 'at my expense' in this context), coming before the verb for emphasis. The adjective روشن modifies روز, while سیاه after them expresses the resulting state ('make [it] black').
segments.s155-g43: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces the subordinate clause meaning 'so that / in order that' (here rendered as 'that'), and دیبه خسروان is the object (the fine silk/robe belonging to the kings) which is the thing ordered to be used.
segments.s155-g44: کشیدند is past plural 'they drew/placed' and takes the prepositional phrase بر روی 'upon/on (the) face' as its complement; پور جوان is a noun phrase 'the son/young prince' with پور 'son' + adjective/epithet جوان 'young'. Word order is verb — preposition — noun phrase, a normal narrative sequence.
segments.s155-g45: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here 'indeed' / 'both') prefacing the coordinated subject آرزوگاه و شهر. آمدش is 3rd-person past/present 'came to him' with enclitic -ش = 'to him'. The coordination links two nouns as the things that 'came'.
segments.s155-g46: یکی is the indefinite/number word 'one'/'a certain' introducing تنگ تابوت 'a narrow coffin' (adjective تنگ before noun تابوت). بهر here functions as 'for / destined for' and آمدش repeats 'came to him' with the enclitic -ش. The whole phrase states that a single narrow coffin arrived/was brought for him.
segments.s155-g47: ازان دشت is a prepositional phrase 'from that plain' (از + آن > ازان). بردند is past plural 'they carried/took' and تابوت اوی is 'his coffin' where اوی is an older third-person possessive/enclitic indicating 'his'. The verb precedes the object as normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s155-g48: سوی 'toward' + خیمه خویش 'their own tent' (خیمه 'tent'; خوی + ش together function as the possessive 'their') marks the direction/place where the coffin was taken. بنهاد is a perfective/past narrative verb 'placed/put down' (the b- prefix is the classical verbal prefix), and روی here means 'upon'/'down' — together 'set it down at/on their tent.'
segments.s155-g105: گر is the classical conditional particle 'if'. ایشان is the third-person plural 'they'. به من marks the beneficiary/target 'to me' (literally 'towards me'). چند here is an indefinite quantifier 'some/a few' modifying بد, which functions as a noun ‘bad deed/harm’. کرده‌اند is the compound perfect (3pl) 'they have done' (کردن used in its basic sense). Word order follows classical Persian: conditional particle + subject + indirect object + quantifier + object + perfective verb.
segments.s155-g106: وگر (و + گر) functions as 'and/or if' — here presenting an alternative conditional 'or if'. دود is the noun 'smoke' as the thing raised; از ایران gives the origin 'from Iran'. برآورده‌اند is the perfect (3pl) of برآوردن 'to bring forth/raise up' and here means 'they have caused (smoke) to rise' (i.e. set ablaze or made smoke appear). The verb appears at the end in typical Persian order.
segments.s155-g107: دل من 'my heart' is the subject. ز (poetic form of از) marks cause 'from/because of' so ز درد تو = 'because of the pain you caused'. شد is the copula/verb 'became'. پر (of) indicates 'full (of)' and is followed again by ز درد to restate/emphasize the fullness 'full of pain'. The phrasing is emphatic/poetic: the cause is stated and the resulting state 'became full of pain' is repeated for effect.
segments.s155-g108: نخواهم is the negative of the present/future auxiliary (ن + خواهم) and gives 'I will not' (classical/fixed auxiliary use of خواستن). از ایشان means 'about/of them' (i.e. concerning those people). همی is an archaic/emphatic particle (here strengthening the statement). یاد کرد is the verbal phrase 'to remember/mention' (یاد as noun + کردن). The clause order places the negative auxiliary before the oblique object and the main verb کرد at the end, a common classical Persian word order.
segments.s155-g17: This is a compact noun phrase: نبیره (scion/offspring) is modified by جهاندارِ سام — a title + name construction meaning “the world-ruler Sam.” سوار is an appositive/adjective placed after the name to say he was mounted/a horseman. The phrase as a whole functions as the subject (or topical NP) of the line.
segments.s155-g18: سوی is a preposition meaning “toward”; مادر is the (understood) mother as the goal. از تخمه نامدار is an origin/qualification phrase: از marks origin/from, تخمه (here: “line/offspring”) + نامدار (“renowned”) describes the mother’s noble descent. Together this gives “toward his mother, of a renowned stock.”
segments.s155-g19: بریدن is the verbal noun (‘the cutting/severing’). دو دستم is the object with possessive -م (“my two hands”). سزاوار هست is a copular predicate meaning “is fitting/appropriate.” The whole clause reads literally as “The cutting of my two hands is fitting.”
segments.s155-g20: جز ... مبادم نشست expresses an optative/negative wish: جز از +ِ محل ('except from') limits the domain, مبادم is the old subjunctive/optative ‘may I not be/let me not’, and نشست (here verb) = ‘sit/settle’. خاک تیره (‘dark earth’) is likely a metaphor for the grave. The clause therefore means roughly “May I not sit except upon the dark earth.”
segments.s155-g21: کدامین پدر is a fronted interrogative NP ‘which/what father’. هرگز here functions as ‘ever’ (in a rhetorical question). این کار کرد is the verb phrase ‘did this deed’ (کرد past tense). The order is typical Persian: question word + noun phrase, then negation/adverb, then verb phrase.
segments.s155-g22: سزاوارم is a predicative adjective with 1st-person clitic (‘it is fitting/appropriate for me’). اکنون (‘now’) sets time. به گفتار سرد is a prepositional phrase of manner: به marks the manner/medium and گفتار سرد = ‘stern/harsh speech’. The clause means “It is fitting for me now to speak in stern words.”
segments.s155-g23: به گیتی (‘in/unto the world’) introduces the setting; که is the relative pronoun ‘that/which’. کشتست is an archaic or contracted perfect/passive-like form ‘has been killed’ (کشت + است) and takes فرزند را as its object (را marks the definite object). The clause reads “the world which has killed the son.”
segments.s155-g24: This is a postpositive apposition/emphatic listing of qualities applied to the previously mentioned object: دلیر و جوان و خردمند را repeats and qualifies ‘the son’ (and the final را reiterates the definiteness/emphasis). It enumerates his attributes: brave, young, and wise.
segments.s155-g57: دریغ here is an interjection/ lament ('what a pity' / 'alas') placed before the noun phrase. این غم و حسرت is a coordinated noun phrase ('this sorrow and longing'); جان گسل is a tight adjective-noun compound meaning 'soul‑breaking' or 'heart‑rending' (جان qualifies گسل). Word order is emphatic/poetic (interjection + demonstrative + coordinated objects + attributive compound).
segments.s155-g58: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing separation. مادر جدا is an adjectival phrase 'separated from (his) mother' (جدا = apart). وز is the fused form of و + ز (and + from) used poetically before پدر; it functions like 'and from'. پدر داغدل is a genitive/attributive sequence 'father, (being) grief‑stricken' (داغدل = heart‑broken).
segments.s155-g59: همی is an archaic/prosaic continuous marker meaning 'kept on / continually'. ریخت here is the verbal root 'poured/was shed' used in narrative (continuous sense); خون is its direct object. The second clause repeats همی + فعل (کند) to indicate another continuous action: کند is the verb 'does/makes' (here 'they make/do' or 'they plough/rend' the خاک), and خاک is its object. The clauses are paratactic (joined by و).
segments.s155-g60: همه جامه خسروی is a nominal phrase 'the entire/whole robe of the king' (همه = whole/entire, جامه = garment, خسروی = royal). کرد چاک is a causative construction: کرد (past 'made/did') plus the noun چاک ('tear, gash') yields 'made (it) torn' — i.e. 'rent/was torn'. The verb کرد is past tense, and the object (چاک) follows, typical of classical poetic ordering.
segments.s155-g61: همه پهلوانان کاووس شاه is a nominal group with apposition/possession: 'all the champions of Kavus the king' (همه = all, پهلوانان = heroes/champions, کاووس شاه = Kavus, the king). In Persian poetic order the ruler's name and title follow the noun they govern.
segments.s155-g62: نشستند is the past‑plural verb 'they sat down' (3rd pl. past of نشستن) describing the action of the preceding noun phrase. بر خاک is the locative adverbial 'on the ground'. با او is 'with him' (accompanying him). به راه is an adverbial phrase literally 'by/at the road' or 'along the way' specifying the setting; together the phrase gives the circumstance of where and with whom they sat.
segments.s155-g63: زبان بزرگان is a genitive/construct phrase 'the speech/tongue of the elders' (زبان = speech; بزرگان = the great ones/elders). پر از پند بود is a predicative clause: پر از = 'full of', پند = 'advice/counsel', and بود is the past copula 'was' — 'was full of advice.' Word order places the copula at the end, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s155-g64: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmatan). به درد is an idiomatic locative/experiential phrase 'in/with pain' (به + درد). از introduces the source/cause 'from / because of' and جگربند is a poetic noun 'beloved / heart‑binder' — so از جگربند بود states the cause of his pain. The clause ends with بود (past 'was').
segments.s155-g49: به introduces a direction/purpose ('to/into') and here marks the object of the action. پرده سرای آتش is a genitive/construct: پردهِ سرایِ آتش = 'the curtain/shroud of the fire‑house/pavilion' (سرای here = 'house/pavilion' in classical usage). اندر is an archaic preposition equivalent to modern در/درِ meaning 'in/into/on', and زدند is the simple past, 3rd person plural ('they struck/they set/they fastened'); together the verb phrase means they set/affixed the shroud onto/over the fire‑pavilion.
segments.s155-g50: همه لشکرش is the subject: 'all his army/host' (همه emphasises totality). خاک بر سر زدند is an idiomatic structure: خاک + بر + سر + زدند = 'to throw dust on one's head' (a gesture of mourning); زدند is past, 3rd person plural 'they did/they struck'. Word order places the idiomatic object (خاک بر سر) before the verb.
segments.s155-g51: همان is a demonstrative ('that/the same') placed before the nouns it qualifies. The phrase pairs two items: خیمه 'tent' and دیبه هفت رنگ 'diabeh seven‑colored' where هفت رنگ (literally 'seven‑colored') follows the noun as an adjectival qualifier — a common classical word order (noun + modifier).
segments.s155-g52: همه here quantifies the following noun phrase ('all' / 'every one of'), with تخت 'couches/thrones/beds' as the head. پرمایه is an adjectival/epithetic word meaning 'rich, substantial, well‑furnished' that follows the noun, and زرین (golden) and پلنگ (here 'leopard/ leopard‑skin' used as an attribute) stack as further adjectives/attributes; classical Persian often places modifiers after the noun in such enumerations.
segments.s155-g53: برآتش is the prepositional phrase 'on/upon the fire' (classical orthography joins the parts), and نهادند is the simple past, 3rd person plural ('they placed/laid'). The clause و برخاست غو inverts the usual order (غو برخاست) so that غو 'uproar' is the subject and برخاست is the intransitive past ('arose'/'arose up'); برخاست is 3rd person singular because غو is grammatically singular.
segments.s155-g54: همی گفت is an archaic periphrasis meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying' (همی marks ongoing/repetitive action in older Persian). گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses do not list the verbal sense, so marked -1). زار‌ای is a lamenting interjection/adverbial manner ('lamentingly' or 'with wailing') followed by a vocative phrase جهاندار نو 'O new sovereign (lord) of the world' — ای is the vocative particle addressing the ruler.
segments.s155-g55: دریغ is an interjection/exclamation 'alas' placed at the start to express pity. آن ('that') points to the following attributes, and the nouns رخ, برز, بالای are listed with و between them — 'face, stature/height, and your high/exalted rank' — with تو 'you' attached at the end, supplying the possessor for the final noun and understood for the prior items as well.
segments.s155-g56: The second clause repeats the exclamation دریغ and the demonstrative آن همه 'all those/so much of' followed by مردی 'manliness/courage' and رای 'counsel/plan' with تو 'your' as the possessor. The construction laments both personal qualities (مردی) and the wisdom or counsel (رای) associated with the addressee.
segments.s155-g33: چه گویم is a rhetorical question: چه ('what') + گویم (1st-person present/subjunctive of گفتن 'to say', literally 'I say' but used as 'what can I say'). چرا introduces the subordinate question 'why'; کشتمش is a past simple verb (کشتم 'I killed') plus the enclitic object −ش 'him/her'. بی‌گناه is a predicate/adjective 'innocent' describing the killed person.
segments.s155-g34: This line inverts normal prose order for poetic effect: چرا روز کردم = 'why did I make (the) day ...' (کردم = I caused/made). برو بر سیاه functions as a verbal phrase meaning 'go/become (upon) black' or more idiomatically 'turn black'; بر is the preposition 'upon/on' and سیاه 'black/dark'. The whole expresses 'why did I make the day dark/black'.
segments.s155-g35: پدرش = پدر + ـش (possessive enclitic 'his/her'); the following adjective phrase آن گرانمایه پهلوان ('that/the esteemed champion') modifies the father. Word order places the possessor + head (پدرش) first, then apposition describing him.
segments.s155-g36: چه گوید is 'what does (he) say' (چه = 'what', گوید = 3rd-person present/subjunctive of گفتن used here as simple 'says' or 'will say'). بدَان is split in the manuscript as ب + دان for metrical reasons: ب = the preposition 'to/for' and دان here stands in for 'آن' ('that'); پاک‌دخت جوان = 'that virtuous/bright maiden, young'. The whole means 'what does he say to that pure young daughter.'
segments.s155-g37: برین is the compound 'بر این' ('upon/on this') written here as بر + ین for the poem's rhythm; تخمه سام = 'the seed/offspring of Sam'. نفرین کنند uses نفرین (a noun 'curse') plus کنند (3rd-person plural of کردن), a habitual/subjunctive form often translated as 'they will utter/lay'—so 'they will curse'.
segments.s155-g38: همه = 'everyone/all', نام من = 'my name' (noun + possessor following it), نیز = 'also', بی‌دین = 'impious/godless' as predicate, and کنند = 3rd-plural 'they make/do' used here to mean 'they will call/regard (as)'. So: 'everyone will also call my name impious.'
segments.s155-g39: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; دانست is the past tense 'he knew' (دانستن). کاین = 'که این' or 'this' (classical demonstrative), کودک ارجمند = 'the esteemed/noble child' as the object of دانست.
segments.s155-g40: بدین سال = 'in this year' (بدین is ب + دین/این for metrical spelling; ب = preposition 'in/with'), گردد is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person of گردیدن 'become' (here 'will become/will grow'), چو = 'like/as', سرو بلند = 'a tall cypress' (simile). The clause means 'would this year become like a tall cypress.'
segments.s155-g65: چنینست is a fixed poetic copula meaning 'such is' or 'it is thus' and heads the clause. کردار is a noun 'deed/conduct' placed before its qualifier (چرخ بلند) in typical Persian modifier order (N + modifier). چرخ here names the cosmic 'Wheel' (fate/sky) and بلند is an adjectival epithet 'high/lofty' modifying چرخ.
segments.s155-g66: This verbless coordinate phrase gives two parallel instrumental phrases introduced by به meaning 'with/in' (here 'with' or 'in the hand of'). دستی is an adverbial/ablative-like word 'by hand; with one hand' and کلاه is the thing held. و simply links the two phrases. The second به introduces دیگر 'the other/another' qualifying کمند 'snare/noose'; the overall sense is 'with one hand a hat, with the other a snare.'
segments.s155-g67: چو is a subordinating particle 'when'. شادان is an adjectival predicate 'joyful' modifying the implicit subject. نشیند (present/subjunctive/optative form of نشستن) means 'sits' or 'is seated' and follows the temporal چو. کسی is the indefinite subject 'someone' and با + کلاه is an adjunct 'with a cap / wearing a cap' describing his condition.
segments.s155-g68: بخم is an archaic/obscure adverbial particle here (poetic, rare) that shades the verb with suddenness or emphasis — note and flag it as archaic. کمندش is the noun 'snare' with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his/its' (i.e. 'his snare' or 'the snare for him'). رباید is the present-tense poetic form of ربودن 'to seize/steal' (third person singular), and ز گاه literally 'from time' i.e. 'at times' or 'from occasion to occasion' — together: 'the snare at times seizes (him)'.
segments.s155-g69: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. مهر is the abstract noun 'love/affection' and باید is the impersonal/modal 'must/ought' expressing necessity. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/still') and بر جهان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the world' that marks the domain of مهر — literally 'Why must love be upon the world?'
segments.s155-g70: چو here functions as 'when'. باید again gives the modal sense 'ought/should'. خرامید is an infinitive or verbal noun form meaning 'to stride/prance proudly' (a poetic verb form). با introduces the accompaniment 'with' and همرهان (an older form of هم‌رهان) means 'companions' — the clause contrasts reigning over the world with what love ought to be doing: striding with companions.
segments.s155-g71: چو is the temporal 'when'. اندیشه is the noun 'thought' or 'reflection'. گنج is used figuratively as 'treasure' (here a metaphorical object of thought). گردد is the present/subjunctive/poetic form of شدن 'to become' (third person singular), so the clause reads 'when thought becomes (a) treasure long (دراز)'. دراز is an adjective 'long/lengthy' modifying گنج.
segments.s155-g72: همی is an emphatic adverb 'indeed/still/then' here linking the result. گشت is a poetic/old form of گشتن/شدن 'to turn; to become' (third person), combined with باید 'must/ought' to express obligation or inevitability. سوی خاک is the directional phrase 'toward the earth' and باز ('again' / 'back') marks return — together: 'it must turn back toward the earth.'
segments.s155-g73: This is a conditional clause: 'اگر' (if) introduces the condition. 'چرخ را' uses the object/topic marker 'را' after 'چرخ' (here 'the heavens/sky' as the topic), and 'هست' is the copula 'is'. 'ازین آگهی' (from/of this information) uses the fused preposition 'از' + demonstrative 'این' (here written 'ین'), so the whole phrase means 'if the heavens are of/aware of this news'. Word order is topical (topic + copula) followed by the prepositional phrase.
segments.s155-g74: 'همانا' is an emphatic adverb meaning 'verily/truly'. 'که' introduces the following clause. 'گشتست' is a perfective/resultative compound meaning 'has become' (classical perf. form). 'مغزش' is 'his brain' (مغز + enclitic ـش), and 'تهی' is the predicate adjective 'empty' placed after the subject; together they mean 'his brain has become empty.'
segments.s155-g75: 'چنان دان' is an imperative/hortative phrase meaning 'consider (it) so' or 'think thus' — 'دان' here functions as 'consider'. 'کزین گردش آگاه نیست' is a subordinate clause: 'کزین' = 'از + این' ('of/from this'), 'گردش' = 'turn/rotation', then 'آگاه نیست' ('is not aware') with the negation expressed by 'نیست' at the verb position.
segments.s155-g76: The conjunction 'که' introduces a result/explanatory clause. 'چون و چرا' is an idiomatic pair meaning 'how and why' (questions). 'سوی او راه نیست' literally 'there is no road/path toward him' — i.e. 'there is no way to approach him with how/why.' The negative 'نیست' appears at the clause end, as typical in Persian.
segments.s155-g77: 'بدین رفتن' (classical form of 'به این رفتن') means 'in/with this going' or 'over this departure' — 'ب' is the prefix 'به', here fused with 'دین' (the orthography is irregular for 'این'); 'رفتن' is the action 'going/departure'. 'اکنون' is the temporal adverb 'now'. 'نباید' is the impersonal modal 'one should not / it must not', followed by the bare verbal form 'گریست' (here functioning as the infinitive 'to weep' in classical usage), so the clause yields 'now one should not weep.'
segments.s155-g78: 'ندانم' is the first-person present-negative 'I do not know'. 'که' introduces the content clause. 'کارش' = 'his affair/deed/matter' (کار + enclitic ـش). 'به فرجام' = 'to the end/outcome' (indicating the endpoint), and the clause closes with the interrogative 'چیست' ('what is it' = 'what is [its] state'). The whole means 'I do not know what his affair will come to.'
segments.s155-g79: 'به رستم' is the indirect object 'to Rostam'. 'چنین گفت' means 'said thus' (chiasmic word order is common in poetry), and 'کاووس' is the speaker's name placed with the verb in a poetic inversion. 'کی' is the interrogative 'who' (here introducing a question in the speech). The line therefore reads 'Kavus thus said to Rostam:
segments.s155-g80: 'که' begins the subordinate clause of the quoted question. 'از ... تا ...' marks a range: 'از کوه البرز تا برگ نی' = 'from the Alborz mountain up to the leaf of the reed' (poetic image). 'تا' here means 'as far as'. The phrase is elliptical — the clause continues in the next line — but grammatically it's a from–to spatial range introduced inside the quotation.
segments.s155-g89: نیابی is the negative present/subjunctive form (ن- + یابی) meaning “you will not / you do not find” and here opens the clause. همان رفته را is the definite object: همان (“the same”) modifies رفته (past participle “gone/departed”) and را is the Persian definite/object marker attached to that noun phrase. باز جای is an adverb+noun phrase “again (باز) [a] place (جای)” that completes the object idea — word order is poetic (verb first, then object phrase).
segments.s155-g90: روانش is روان (“soul, spirit”) with the third-person possessive suffix -ش = “his/her soul,” functioning as the subject. کهن شد is past copular construction: کهن (“old/ancient”) + شد (past of شدن, “became”), here idiomatically indicating the soul’s change of place/state. به دیگر سرای is a prepositional phrase “into/ to another (دیگر) abode (سرای),” where به introduces the destination; note سرای’s usual sense “abode/house” (sense not listed in the provided senses).
segments.s155-g91: من از دور دیدم is a simple past clause: subject من (“I”) + adverbial از دور (“from afar”) + verb دیدم (“I saw,” past). بر و یال اوی is the direct object: two coordinated nouns (بر and یال “mane”) joined by و, with the possessive enclitic اوی (“his”) attached after the coordinated phrase — a common poetic placement of the possessive.
segments.s155-g92: چنان is an adverb “such/so,” qualifying what follows. برز و بالا are descriptive nouns/adjectives (“exalted/high, height/loftiness”) linked by و; they describe the subject seen. گوپال اوی is a name/epithet (گوپال) with the same possessive enclitic اوی (“his”) applying to the whole descriptive string — in poetry the final possessive often attaches to the last element while referring back to the subject. Word order places descriptors after the object for emphasis.
segments.s155-g93: زمانه is the agent/subject “time/fortune.” برانگیختش is the past verb برانگیخت(ن) with the enclitic -ش = “roused him” (causative/active past). با سپاه is an instrumental/accompaniment prepositional phrase (“with an army”), indicating how/with what he was roused. The verb is causative past and the object is cliticized to the verb.
segments.s155-g94: که introduces the subordinate/result clause “that/so that.” ایدر is the adverb “here.” به دست تو is an agent/instrument phrase literally “by the hand of you,” where به + دست expresses agency/power and تو is the 2nd-person pronoun. گردد is the present/subjunctive of شدن used here to express resultant/future state “become / be brought (to),” and تباه is the predicate adjective “ruined.” The clause therefore means “that here he will/become ruined by your hand.”
segments.s155-g95: This is a two-part interrogative: چه سازی و درمان is a parallel compound meaning “what stratagem/means (چه سازی) and remedy/cure (درمان).” این کار is the bound demonstrative + noun “this affair.” چیست is the copula (چی + ست = “what is”), so the whole line asks “What means or remedy is there for this affair?” Note the rhetorical pairing of سازی and درمان to ask “what will you do?/what cure exists.”
segments.s155-g96: برین رفته is a short fused phrase equivalent to بر این رفته: بر این (“regarding this”) + رفته (past participle “gone/departed”) giving “for/on this departed one.” تا چند is the duration question “until how long / how long.” خواهی گریست is the periphrastic future/intentional form: خواهی (second-person auxiliary “you will/would”) + گریست (root “weep/lament”) = “will you weep.” Altogether: “How long will you keep weeping for the departed?”
segments.s155-g25: نکوهش فراوان is a noun phrase 'much reproach' (نکوهش = reproach, فراوان = abundant/plentiful); کند is the verbal present 'does/utters' (3rd‑person singular) placed after the object phrase in poetic word order; زال زر is the subject with an epithetic post‑modifier (Zal + زر 'gold' as an epithet = 'Zal the Golden').
segments.s155-g26: همان نیز = 'that same ... also' (همان as demonstrative 'that/the same', نیز = 'also'); رودابه پرهنر is the proper name with an adjectival epithet ('Rudabeh, skilled'). The segment links to the previous clause by pointing to another speaker/actor.
segments.s155-g27: بدین کار = 'for/by this deed' (بدین = 'به + این', 'by this'; کار = 'deed/act'); پوزش 'apology/excuse' is the thing asked about; چه پیش آورم is a rhetorical question 'what shall I bring forward/present?' where پیش آوردن = 'to bring forward/present' and آورم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I might bring / should I bring'.
segments.s155-g28: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'so that / in order that'; دل‌شان = 'their hearts' (دل + possessive ـشان) is the semantic object of the intended action; به گفتار خویش آورم = 'I may bring/turn (their) hearts to my speech/words' (به = 'to/toward', گفتار = 'speech', خویش + ش = 'own/their own' or here 'my own' depending on scope; آورم again = 1st‑person 'I may bring').
segments.s155-g29: چه at clause‑start is an interrogative 'what'; گویند here functions as the verb 'they say/will say' (3rd‑person plural, an archaic/poetic present form); گردان و گردنکشان are the plural subjects 'the troops and the rebellious/proud ones'.
segments.s155-g30: چو = 'when/once'; زین سان is a fixed poetic phrase 'in this manner/this way' (زین = 'this', سان = 'manner'); شود is 3rd‑person singular 'becomes/happens'; نزد ایشان نشان = 'a sign/mark before them' (نزد = 'in the presence of/for', ایشان = 'them', نشان = 'sign'). The whole clause: 'when in this way it becomes a sign for them.'
segments.s155-g31: چه گویم = 'what shall I say' (چه = interrogative 'what', گویم = 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I say/should say'); چو = 'when'; آگه شود = 'becomes aware/notified' (آگه = 'aware', شود = 'becomes'); مادرش = 'his/her mother' (مادر + possessive ـش). The clause asks rhetorically what one can say once the mother learns of it.
segments.s155-g32: چه گونه = 'in what way/how' (چه = 'what', گونه = 'manner/way'); فرستم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'shall I send/dispatch'; کسی را is the object 'someone' with the definite/object marker را; برش is a short phrase meaning 'to/at him' or similar in context (here used idiomatically 'to him/against him') — the segment asks how one could send someone to him. (Note: برش is a compressed/poetic form; its literal senses listed do not match this pronominal/locative use exactly.)
segments.s155-g81: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning “still/continually.” برد here is the verbal root used with the future auxiliary خواهد to mean “carry/take” (the exact verbal sense is classical/poetic). خواهد is the future/auxiliary “will.” به گردش سپهر is a prepositional phrase showing the means/place: به + گردش (the rotation/turning) + سپهر (the heavens). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (adverb + verb + auxiliary).
segments.s155-g82: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning “one must not/should not.” فگندن is the infinitive “to throw/cast,” governed by نباید (i.e. “should not cast”). بدین is the prepositional phrase composed of ب (به → “to/into”) + دین (here functioning as demonstrative ‘this’ in the fused poetic form بدین = “to/into this”), followed by خاک مهر = “the earth/soil of affection.” The whole clause means “one must not cast/throw affection into this earth.”
segments.s155-g83: یکی زود سازد is an elliptical/poetic clause: یکی = “one/some,” زود = “early/soon,” and سازد (from ساختن) = “makes/achieves/comes about.” The second یکی دیرتر parallels the first: “one (is) sooner, one later.” The verb سازد is used transitively but the object is omitted—this is a general observation about different timings.
segments.s155-g84: سرانجام is an adverb/noun meaning “in the end/finally.” بر مرگ is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward/on/against death” (بر marks direction/tendency). باشد is a classical copular/modal form here functioning as “is” (optative/old-fashioned present), and گذر is the noun “passage/passing.” The clause therefore states the general conclusion: “in the end the passing is toward death.”
segments.s155-g85: تو is the vocative/subject “you.” دل را is the direct object marked by را (“your heart”). بدین رفته is a prepositional/attributive phrase: بدین (به + این, “with/to this”) + رفته (past participle “gone/departed”), meaning “with this departed (one)/about what has gone.” خرسند کن is an imperative “make (it) content/pleased.” Overall: “You, make your heart content with what has gone.”
segments.s155-g86: همه گوش is a noun phrase meaning “all (people)’s ear(s)” i.e. “everyone’s attention.” سوی marks direction “toward,” خردمند is the target “the wise (person),” and کن is the imperative “make/do.” The idiom همه گوش سوی ... کن = “make everyone turn their ears/attention to the wise” (i.e. listen to the wise).
segments.s155-g87: اگر is the conditional “if.” آسمان بر زمین is the phrase “the sky upon the earth” (بر = “on/upon”), and بر زنی is a verbal construction where زنی is the 2nd-person singular present subjunctive/imperative-like form of زدن “to strike/put” (classical/poetic usage), meaning “you should/you would put/strike.” The clause is a hypothetical exaggeration: “if you put/lay heaven upon earth.”
segments.s155-g88: وگر is the alternate conditional “or if” (but if). آتش اندر جهان is “fire within the world” (اندر = “in/within”), در is the prepositional/verb-prefixed particle combining with زنی (again the 2nd-person form of زدن) so that در زنی = “you put/you set (in).” The whole clause: “or if you set fire in the world.” The verbal forms are archaic/poetic second-person forms.
segments.s155-g97: بدو (to him) is a dative/adverbial prepositional form placed before the verb; گفت is the simple past 'said' (classical narrative order). که introduces a reported clause. او خود گذشت is the reported clause where او 'he' + خود is emphatic 'himself' and گذشت is a past action 'passed/left' (here 'had gone on' or 'departed').
segments.s155-g98: نشستست is an archaic 3rd‑person past form 'sat/was seated' and appears before its subject (typical older Persian word order). هومان is the subject/name; درین = در + این 'in this' (ین here is the orthographic variant of 'این'), پهن دشت is the locative noun phrase 'wide plain'.
segments.s155-g99: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; توران names the region. سرانند is an older/compound form meaning 'are [chiefs/leaders]' (سران + اند = 'they are leaders'). و چندی ز چین adds 'and some from China' (چندی = 'some').
segments.s155-g100: از ایشان = 'from them' (از + pronominal -شان). بدل here denotes 'retaliation' or 'retribution'. در مدار is a figurative locative 'in the sphere/realm of', ایچ is an older negative/privative ('no', 'not any'), and کین means 'vengeance/hatred' — the clause says there is no retaliatory hatred among them (no reciprocated enmity).
segments.s155-g101: زواره is a place name indicating destination. سپه را is the definite object 'the army' marked by the object marker را. گذارد is the past transitive 'placed/sent', and به راه 'on the road' / 'set in motion' indicates direction or that the army was put on the march.
segments.s155-g102: به here marks instrument or cause 'by/with'; نیروی یزدان is a genitive phrase 'the power/strength of God' (نیروی = 'power/strength'), and فرمان شاه is coordinated by و 'and' to give 'and the king's command' — together they specify the authority/occasion for sending the army.
segments.s155-g103: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', placed before the verb). شاه‌ای is the subject 'the king' (شاه) with the vocative-like particle -ای appearing in speech; گو is an imperative 'say/speak' addressing the addressee, and نامجوی is a vocative or descriptive title 'seeker of a name/renown' (here used as an epithet). The whole unit introduces the king's direct address.
segments.s155-g104: ازین = از + این 'from this', رزم 'battle/combat'. اندوهت is the noun اندوه 'sorrow' with 2nd‑person possessive suffix -ت 'your sorrow'. آید is the present/future-oriented verb 'comes/will come'. به روی is a locative 'upon the face' — literally 'from this battle your sorrow will come upon (your) face', i.e., 'will sorrow show on your face'.
segments.s155-g9: پدر جست is a simple past clause: پدر (father) as subject followed by جست (past of جهیدن, 'sprang up'). The coordinate conjunction و links the next clause: برزد یکی سرد باد is VSO/archaic word order where برزد (an archaic verb meaning 'struck/fell/came') precedes its subject. یکی ('one/a') quantifies باد ('wind'), and سرد is an adjectival modifier placed before باد for poetic effect (word order inversion).
segments.s155-g10: بنالید is the past tense 'he moaned/wailed'. The conjunction و simply links the following action. مژگان به هم بر نهاد is a light/periphrastic clause: مژگان 'eyelashes' is the object, به هم 'toward each other/together' gives the manner, بر (here 'upon') + نهاد (past of نهادن, 'put/placed') together form the compound verb 'put together' = 'closed/pressed together' (i.e., closed his eyelids). Note the elided subject (the father) continues across both verbs.
segments.s155-g11: همی گفت is a habitual/progressive past: همی ('still/continually') + گفت (said) = 'he kept saying' or 'he said repeatedly'. زار‌ای is an interjection of lament (زار used as an exclamation 'alas! / woe!'). نبرده is a participial/archaic form used adjectivally here (literally 'not taken' or 'not conquered') to mean 'unsubdued/unconquered' modifying جوان ('young man'). Note classical Persian often uses participles where Modern Persian would use adjectives or relative clauses.
segments.s155-g12: سرافراز is an epithet 'honored/lofty/triumphant' further characterizing the young man. The coordinating و links the next descriptive phrase. از تخمه پهلوان is a genitival phrase: از ('of/from') + تخمه ('offspring/stock') + پهلوان ('champion, hero') = 'of the stock of champions' or 'sprung from a heroic line.' The sequence is a compact, poetic way to give lineage/epithet.
segments.s155-g13: نبیند is an impersonal/3rd-person negative present (from دیدن) meaning 'is not seen' or 'one does not see'. چو تو نیز ('like you also') places تو (you) in the comparison role: چو = 'like/as', نیز = 'also/too'. خورشید و ماه are the two classical high comparisons ('sun and moon'); the clause says nothing like you is seen even among these celestial paragons. Note the verb precedes the similes (classical/poetic ordering).
segments.s155-g14: This is a correlative negative list introduced by repeated نه ... نه ... ('neither ... nor ...'): نه جوشن (no cuirass/armor), نه تخت (no throne), و نه تاج و کلاه (nor crown nor cap). Items are listed in parallel; the repeated نه gives an emphatic negation — 'there is none like you among armor, throne, crown, or cap.'
segments.s155-g15: This segment contains archaic/interrogative elements. کرا is an archaic interrogative 'who'. آمد is the past 'came'. این پیش is the adverbial phrase 'here/into this presence' (این = 'this', پیش = 'before/presence'). کامد is an archaic present-form of 'to come/arrive' used adjectivally or as part of a clause ('whoever comes/has come'); م here is an enclitic/old form meaning 'is' or signals existence in classical usage, and را is the definite-object/ topicalizing postposition attached to whatever is object-marked. In sum the clause asks/remarks about who has come into this presence — classical word order and particles make the exact modern rendering compact and elliptical.
segments.s155-g16: بکشتم is an archaic/periphrastic past first-person form meaning 'I killed' or 'I caused to be killed' (classical 'ب' prefix + past personal ending). جوانی is the direct object 'a young man' (indefinite). به پیران سرا is a locative phrase: به here means 'in/at' (poetic locative), پیران 'the elders' (genitive/attributive), and سرا 'house/place' — 'in the elders' house.' The clause therefore gives the speaker's action and its location.
segments.s155-g1: بفرمود رستم که تا پیشکار is a clause-head: بفرمود (past, honorific) is the main verb 'he commanded/said'; رستم is the subject following the verb (typical poetic order). که introduces the subordinate content clause. تا پیشکار here marks the indirect/target person (the attendant) of the order; تا is being used in a purposive/targeting sense with پیشکار ('the attendant').
segments.s155-g2: یکی جامه افگند بر جویبار is the content of the order. افگند is a simple past 'threw/cast' and is the finite verb of the subordinate clause; one can read the clause as an imperative reported indirectly ('that (he) cast/should cast'). یکی is indefinite here ('someone' / 'a certain one') and جامه is the object 'a robe/garment'; بر + جویبار places the action onto/into the stream (بر marks direction/placement).
segments.s155-g3: جوان را بران جامه آن جایگاه bundles several modifiers before the verb. جوان + را marks 'the young man' as a definite direct object. بران is an archaic/poetic contraction meaning 'بر آن' ('on that') and modifies جامه ('on that garment'); آن جایگاه further specifies the place ('that spot'). The whole phrase is the object-complex for the following verb (i.e., 'he laid the young man on that garment in that place').
segments.s155-g4: بخوابید و آمد به نزدیک شاه contains two coordinated past actions. بخوابید here functions transitively with the preceding را-marked object: '(he) put/laid (him) to rest / laid him down' (past). و joins the next action; آمد (past 'came') with به نزدیک شاه ('to near the king') indicates motion toward the king. Subject is implicit and continues from the prior clause (the attendant or the person carrying out Rostam's order).
segments.s155-g5: گو پیلتن سر سوی راه کرد is a compact scene-setting clause. گو here functions as a poetic introductory particle attached to the place name پیلتن (so the pair reads like 'at Piltan' / 'there at Piltan'); سر سوی راه کرد is literal: 'turned (his) head toward the road' (سر = head, سوی = toward, کرد is the past auxiliary/ lexical 'did' combining with the phrase to mean 'turned'). Word order is subject/location + verb phrase, typical of narrative verse.
segments.s155-g6: کس آمد پسش زود و آگاه کرد is a sequence: کس آمد ('someone came'), پسش ('after him' / 'afterward') modifies the arrival, زود adverbially marks the speed ('quickly/soon'), و links the next action, and آگاه کرد is a light-verb construction meaning 'made (him) aware' / 'informed (him)'. The agent of آگاه کردن is the same 'someone' who arrived (implicit subject).
segments.s155-g7: که سهراب شد زین جهان فراخ: که introduces the reported content/reason. سهراب شد is past 'Sohrab became' / 'Sohrab was' — in context it conveys that Sohrab is to leave or is separated from life. زین here is not the common noun 'saddle' but an older prepositional contraction (z + in = 'from this'); because that sense was not in the provided list, it is treated as an archaic prepositional form meaning 'from this/this very'. جهان فراخ 'the wide/broad world' is the object of that prepositional phrase.
segments.s155-g8: همی از تو تابوت خواهد نه کاخ states the consequence: همی is an emphatic particle ('indeed' / 'surely') and خواهد is a future/simple modal 'will want/require' (here 'will take from you'); از تو marks source/possession 'from you'; تابوت is 'coffin' and کاخ 'palace' contrasts what will be taken. The negative contrast 'نه کاخ' (not a palace) is a succinct poetic antithesis: he will take your coffin, not your palace.

sentences.s155-s21: A plan comes to him to go to war and he raises an army; he will snatch my bright day and make it dark.
sentences.s155-s22: He commanded that they draw the kings' silk robe, and they spread it over the young prince's face.
sentences.s155-s23: Both the beloved's dwelling and the city came upon him; a single narrow coffin came to him.
sentences.s155-s24: From that plain they carried his coffin and set it down by their own tent.
sentences.s155-s53: If they have done some wrong to me, or if they have raised smoke from Iran.
sentences.s155-s54: My heart became full of sorrow because of you; I will not remember them.
sentences.s155-s9: Sam's scion, mounted, rode toward his mother, who was of a renowned line.
sentences.s155-s10: The cutting off of my two hands is fitting; may I not sit except upon the dark earth.
sentences.s155-s11: Which father ever did this deed? It is fitting for me now to speak in stern words.
sentences.s155-s12: In a world that has killed the son — the brave, the young, the wise.
sentences.s155-s29: Alas — this soul‑rending sorrow and yearning, separated from his mother and with his father heart‑broken.
sentences.s155-s30: Blood continued to pour, and they kept rending the earth; the king's entire robe was torn.
sentences.s155-s31: All the champions of King Kavus sat down on the ground with him by the road.
sentences.s155-s32: The speech of the elders was full of counsel; Tahmatan, because of his beloved, was in pain.
sentences.s155-s25: They hung a shroud over the pavilion of the fire, and all his host threw dust upon their heads.
sentences.s155-s26: That same tent and seven‑colored brocade; and all the couches, richly furnished, golden and with leopard‑skins.
sentences.s155-s27: They placed it upon the fire, and an uproar arose; they kept saying, lamenting, 'O new sovereign of the world!'
sentences.s155-s28: Alas for that face and your height and your exalted station; alas for all that manhood and your counsel.
sentences.s155-s17: What can I say? Why did I kill him when he was innocent? Why did I make the day turn black?
sentences.s155-s18: Her father, that noble champion—what does he say to that fair young maiden?
sentences.s155-s19: They will curse this seed/line of Sam; everyone will also call my name godless.
sentences.s155-s20: For he knew that this noble child would, in this year, grow up like a tall cypress.
sentences.s155-s33: Such is the conduct of the high Wheel: in one hand a cap, in the other a snare.
sentences.s155-s34: When someone sits joyful wearing a cap, at times the snare suddenly seizes him.
sentences.s155-s35: Why must love prevail over the world, when it should be striding along with companions?
sentences.s155-s36: When thought becomes a long treasure, it must then turn back toward the earth.
sentences.s155-s37: If the heavens know of this news, truly his brain has become empty.
sentences.s155-s38: Consider him thus: he is not aware of this turning, so that there is no way to him for asking how or why.
sentences.s155-s39: One should not weep now over this departure; I do not know what his affair's outcome is.
sentences.s155-s40: Kavus thus said to Rostam: «Who is it that, from the Alborz mountain to the leaf of the reed...»
sentences.s155-s45: You will not find again the same place of the one who has gone; his soul has gone into another abode.
sentences.s155-s46: From afar I saw his fore (side) and mane; so tall and lofty was he, and Gopāl was his.
sentences.s155-s47: Fortune roused him with an army, so that here he might be brought to ruin by your hand.
sentences.s155-s48: What device or remedy is there for this affair? How long will you keep weeping for the one who has gone?
sentences.s155-s13: Zal the Golden spoke much reproach; likewise the skilled Rudabeh.
sentences.s155-s14: What apology can I present for this deed, so that I may win their hearts to my words?
sentences.s155-s15: What will the troops and the rebels say when this comes to be a sign for them?
sentences.s155-s16: What shall I say when his mother becomes aware? In what manner shall I send someone to him?
sentences.s155-s41: The turning heavens will still carry (him) along; one should not cast affection into this earth.
sentences.s155-s42: Some (come) early, others later; in the end the passage is toward death.
sentences.s155-s43: Make your heart content with this one who has gone; turn everyone's ear toward the wise.
sentences.s155-s44: If you were to set the heavens upon the earth, or if you were to kindle fire within the world...
sentences.s155-s49: Rostam said to him that he himself had gone on; Homan sat in this wide plain.
sentences.s155-s50: They are chiefs from Turan, and some are from China; from them there is no retaliation in the sphere of enmity.
sentences.s155-s51: He put the army on the road to Zowareh by the power of God and by the king's command.
sentences.s155-s52: The king said to him, 'O seeker of renown, speak — will sorrow from this battle come upon your face?'
sentences.s155-s5: The father sprang up; a cold wind struck — he moaned and drew his lashes together.
sentences.s155-s6: He wailed, saying, 'Alas! O unconquered young man, proud and sprung from the stock of champions.'
sentences.s155-s7: One does not see, like you, sun or moon; nor armor, nor throne, nor crown or cap.
sentences.s155-s8: Who came here before? He came into my presence; I slew a young man in the elders' house.
sentences.s155-s1: Rostam commanded that the attendant should throw a robe into the stream.
sentences.s155-s2: He laid the young man upon that garment in that place, then came up near the king.
sentences.s155-s3: At (the place called) Piltan he turned his head toward the road; someone quickly came after him and informed (the king).
sentences.s155-s4: That Sohrab would leave this wide world — he will take from you a coffin, not a palace.
